coredata.dc:title: Enhanced overall water splitting by CQDs-coupled RuO2-IrO2 heterojunction in acidic media

coredata.dc:creator: Su, Yuwen
Wu, Han
Wang, Siyang
Hu, Zhiang
Li, Jian
Chang, Jiangwei
Yin, Guangchao
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: The development of highly active and stable bifunctional electrocatalysts in acidic media is crucial to hydrogen production by proton exchange membrane. In this study, we designed a RuO2-IrO2 heterostructure catalyst coupled by carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The catalyst showed excellent electrocatalytic performance for water splitting under acidic conditions. The overpotentials of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) were as low as 180 and 15 mV at 10 mA/cm2 in 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. The acid electrolytic cell developed with RuO2-IrO2@CQDs as anode and cathode operated stably at 10 mA/cm2 for 120 h. In situ measurements and theoretical calculation reveal that the unique lattice oxygen mechanism path of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs can bypass the OOH* intermediate and breaks the linear relationship of adsorbent evolution mechanism path, resulting in higher OER catalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass CQDs
RuO2-IrO2 heterojunction
Multiple active sites
Acidic overall water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs towards acidic OER
Electrocatalytic performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs towards acidic HER
Overall water splitting in acidic media

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ZR2024MB087, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52122308, 51973200, 52202050, and 21905253), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (202300410372), the Joint Fund of Science and Technology R&D Plan of Henan Province (232301420042) and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022TQ0286). We also acknowledge the Center for Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing of Zhengzhou University for supporting this project. All the data for this study can be found in the article and Supporting Information.

This work was partly supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ZR2024MB087, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52122308, 51973200, 52202050, and 21905253), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (202300410372), the Joint Fund of Science and Technology R&D Plan of Henan Province (232301420042) and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022TQ0286). We also acknowledge the Center for Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing of Zhengzhou University for supporting this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs. (b) XRD pattern of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, and IrO2@CQDs. (c) Elemental mapping images of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs. (d) C 1s & Ru 3d, (e) Ir 4f, and (f) O 1s XPS spectra of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and RuO2-IrO2.

(a) LSV curves for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and Com. IrO2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. (b) TOF curves of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and Com. IrO2. (c) Comparison of the ECSAs and mass activities of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and Com. IrO2. In situ EIS spectra measurements of measured (d) RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, (e) RuO2@CQDs, and (f) RuO2@CQDs from 1.25 to 1.60 V (vs. RHE). (g) Overpotential and Tafel slope of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and Com. IrO2. (h) Comparison of the overpotential and Tafel slope of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs with other recently reported high-performance OER catalysts.

(a) In situ DEMS signals of 32O2, 34O2, and 36O2 varied with time when the LSV test of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs (18O-labelled) was performed in H2 16O electrolyte at 1.2–1.6 V (vs. RHE). (b) Detailed intensity comparison of 32O2, 34O2, and 36O2 signals in (a). (c) Ratios of 34O2:32O2 from (b). (d) Schematic illustration of the in situ ATR-SEIRAS IR apparatus. (e) In situ ATR-SEIRAS IR analyses spectroscopic investigation for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs during acidic OER. (f) Proposed LOM path on RuO2-IrO2@CQDs under acidic conditions. (g) ΔG diagrams of OER via LOM and AEM pathways on RuO2-IrO2@CQDs at U = 0 V versus RHE. (h) Comparison of the ΔG in RDS based on LOM and AEM.

(a) Representative LSV curves for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4. (b) TOF curves of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C. (c) Comparison of the ECSAs and mass activities of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C. (d) Tafel slope of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C. (e) C dl curves of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C. (f) EIS data for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C. (g) Overpotential and Tafel slope of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and 20% Pt/C. (h) Comparison of the overpotential and Tafel slope of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs with other recently reported high-performance HER catalysts.

(a) Overall water splitting polarisation curves for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs and 20% Pt/C-IrO2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. In situ EIS spectra measurements of measured (b) RuO2-IrO2@CQDs and (c) 20% Pt/C-IrO2 from 1.25 to 1.60 V (vs. RHE). (d) Chronopotentiometry test for overall water splitting performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs and 20% Pt/C-IrO2. The inset in (d) is a photograph of a homemade H-type cell. (e) Comparison of the cell voltage and stability of an electrolyser based on RuO2-IrO2@CQDs with recently reported electrocatalysts at 10 mA/cm2. (f) Polarization curve of the PEM electrolyzer using RuO2-IrO2@CQDs as anode and cathode electrocatalysts obtained at 80 °C with Nafion 115 membrane. (g) Chronopotentiometry curve of the assembled PEM electrolyzer operated at 1 A/cm2.

head.ce:title: Enhanced overall water splitting by CQDs-coupled RuO2-IrO2 heterojunction in acidic media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pingyuan Laboratory, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, Henan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pingyuan Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pingyuan Laboratory, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, Henan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-quality RuO2-IrO2 heterostructure regulated by carbon quantum dots (CQDs), combined with the unique lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM), enables the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst to exhibit excellent catalytic activity and stability for overall water splitting under acidic conditions.

The development of highly active and stable bifunctional electrocatalysts in acidic media is crucial to hydrogen production by proton exchange membrane. In this study, we designed a RuO2-IrO2 heterostructure catalyst coupled by carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The catalyst showed excellent electrocatalytic performance for water splitting under acidic conditions. The overpotentials of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) were as low as 180 and 15 mV at 10 mA/cm2 in 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. The acid electrolytic cell developed with RuO2-IrO2@CQDs as anode and cathode operated stably at 10 mA/cm2 for 120 h. In situ measurements and theoretical calculation reveal that the unique lattice oxygen mechanism path of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs can bypass the OOH* intermediate and breaks the linear relationship of adsorbent evolution mechanism path, resulting in higher OER catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Among sustainable clean energy sources, hydrogen (H2) is one of the greenest and cleanest energy sources, and water electrolysis is one of the most viable means to produce H2, contributing ∼4% of the global H2 production [1,2]. The process of water electrolysis includes two half reactions, oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis in acidic electrolytes has considerable advantages over that in alkaline electrolytes, including higher voltage efficiency, lower gas purity requirements, and more compact system design [3,4]. To achieve overall electrolysis in an integrated electrolyser, using a PEM electrolysis cell to couple OER and HER in an acidic environment is vital. However, owing to the slow kinetics of OER and HER, the overall water splitting efficiency is severely limited, stimulating the demand for high-performance catalysts [5–14]. Although several non-precious metals can efficiently perform OER and HER in alkaline environments [15–27], they often undergo severe dissolution in harsh acidic environments. Therefore, the design of catalysts should be based on precious-metal electrocatalysts with high corrosion resistance and activity. However, Pt/C catalyst with good HER activity in acidic solutions is known to have high price and exhibit poor OER activity. IrO2 has high corrosion resistance as a commercial OER electrocatalyst, but its high price and low OER activity have hindered its large-scale application. These limitations stimulated our research to improve the noble metal catalyst in the field of overall water splitting.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of low-cost, non-toxic, and pollution-free material with advantages such as large specific surface area, excellent charge transfer ability, high conductivity, and abundant surface functional groups (–COOH, –OH, etc.) [28–34]. Notably, CQDs can form stable CQD-metal ion composite materials with metal ions, and the surrounding CQDs can effectively inhibit the aggregation and oxidation of metal nanoparticles due to the strong interaction between metal and CQDs [35,36]. CQDs can form a CQDs-metal interface effect between CQDs and metals, and these interfaces usually show a strong electron metal supported interaction (EMSI) value in electrocatalysis, which makes the catalyst have a unique catalytic behavior [37]. Recently, it was reported that RuCo@CQDs catalyst was constructed using CQDs [38]. It was observed that CQDs promoted the electron transfer inside the catalyst, which could promote the electron transfer from Ru to CQDs, thus increasing the content of high-priced Ru and enhancing its catalytic activity. Therefore, CQDs can serve as good building blocks for coupling different metal ions to form heterogeneous structures, which can provide a promising route for the efficient development of metal/carbon electrocatalysts.

For OER, it is crucial to understand the reaction mechanism for catalyst design and optimization. The predominant mechanism governing the OER is the adsorbent evolution mechanism (AEM); however, owing to the limited binding strength of OER intermediates at the active sites, the theoretical minimum overpotential is constrained to 0.37 V [39]. To break this limitation, a lattice oxygen mechanism (LOM) has been proposed, which uses the abundant lattice oxygen in metal oxides to generate oxygen. A recent work from isotope-labeled mass spectrometry experiments has directly demonstrated that lattice oxygen in metal oxides can directly generate oxygen [40], and multiple density functional theory (DFT) has demonstrated that LOM indeed requires less overpotential than AEM [41].

Herein, we developed a type of CQDs prepared from heather leaves. A RuO2-IrO2@CQDs hybrid catalyst with a heterogeneous structure was prepared using simple hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods to endow the catalyst with an electronic structure and charge distribution that were favourable for OER and HER. Results showed that the prepared RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst had excellent catalytic activity in acidic media. The OER and HER at 10 mA/cm2 of current density exhibited improved performance, requiring only 180 and 15 mV overpotentials, respectively. This is in stark contrast to the observations for commercial (Com.) IrO2 and 20% Pt/C catalysts, which demand higher overpotentials (367 and 26 mV), and most electrocatalysts reported in acidic media in recent years. Furthermore, when the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst was assembled into a two-electrode electrolytic cell, an extremely low cell voltage of 1.43 V was required to achieve a current density of 10 mA/cm2. This cell voltage is considerably lower than that required for the overall water splitting of 20% Pt/C-IrO2 (1.62 V) and most bifunctional electrocatalysts reported in recent years in acidic media. In addition, we proposed an alternative LOM that can break the linear scale of the traditional adsorbate evolution mechanism, which is critical for improving OER performance.
Details of the experiment are in the Supporting Information.

CQDs were synthesised using a simple hydrothermal method from heather leaves through dehydration and carbonisation. The morphology of the synthesised CQDs was characterised via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images (Fig. S1a and b) show the uniform dispersion of the synthesised CQDs. According to the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. S1b), the lattice fringe with a layer spacing of 0.207 nm is attributed to the (100) crystal face of graphitic carbon [32]. In addition, X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the synthesised CQDs (Fig. S2) shows a wide characteristic absorption peak at 20.6°, which is a typical feature of CQDs [42]. The stretching vibrations of –OH, C=O, C–H, C–O–C, and other functional groups on the surface of the CQDs can be observed from Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. S3), which indicates the formation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures. The optical properties of the synthesised CQDs were investigated via ultraviolet-visible light (UV-vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies (Figs. S4 and S5). The UV-vis spectrum has an absorption band at about 270 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of the C=C bond and the n → π* transition of the sp 2 hybrid C=O bonds. According to the PL spectra, the CQDs emit blue light at 500 nm and exhibit conspicuous excitation wavelength-dependent PL. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the surface elemental composition and chemical state of the CQDs (Fig. S6). The XPS spectrum (Fig. S6a) indicates that the surface of the CQDs mainly comprises C and O. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. S6(b) can be deconvolved into three peaks: C–C, C–O/C–O–C, and C=O peaks. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum shown in Fig. S6(c) comprises two peaks corresponding to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C.

Owing to the small size, large surface area, and excellent electron transfer ability of CQDs, they are expected to be used as a good construction unit coupling metal to improve catalytic performance. In this study, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs were successfully synthesised and used as a highly efficient catalyst for water splitting under acidic conditions. In particular, CQDs were first combined with metal ions (Ru3+ and Ir3+) using a simply hydrothermal method. Subsequently, annealing was used to promote the nucleation and growth of metallic oxide nanoparticle and the formation of heterostructure. The morphology of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs was observed via TEM. Fig. S7 clearly shows the uniform distribution of RuO2 and IrO2 nanoparticles on the CQDs matrix. The HRTEM images (Fig. 1 a) show numerous clear interfaces between RuO2 and IrO2 nanoparticles. At these interfaces, the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.314 nm matches the (110) crystal face of IrO2, while the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.254 nm belongs to the (101) crystal face of RuO2. This observation confirms that RuO2-IrO2@CQDs have a polycrystalline structure comprising IrO2 and RuO2. In addition, the distribution diagram of the elements in the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra (Fig. 1c) shows the uniform distribution of C, O, Ru, and Ir and no obvious agglomeration. The catalyst material was tested via XRD to elucidate its crystal structure. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the peak position of the catalyst material appears between the RuO2 and IrO2 phases; with an increase in Ir content, the material crystallinity decreases and shifts towards IrO2, confirming the formation of RuO2-IrO2 heterostructure.

The chemical states of various elements of catalysts were analyzed via XPS (Fig. 1d–f) and Fig. S8 indicated that all elements on the surface of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs were present, which confirmed the successful preparation of the catalyst. Some studies under acidic conditions have shown that Ru has a stronger bonding strength with H2 than Ir with H2, both of which are not conducive to HER [43]. Fig. 1(d) shows the Ru 3d spectra of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, RuO2@CQDs, and RuO2-IrO2, where the peaks with binding energy (BE) near 281 and 282.1 eV are attributed to Ru 3d and its oscillating satellite peaks, respectively [44]. In addition, the Ir 4f core energy level region of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and RuO2-IrO2 (Fig. 1e) shows two sets of peaks, attributable to Ir4+ and Ir3+, respectively [45]. In particular, the Ru 3d BE of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs moves 0.12 eV in the forward direction compared with RuO2@CQDs, indicating that Ir injection affects the electronic structure of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs. Moreover, the Ir spectrum of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs negatively shifts by 0.7 eV compared with that of IrO2@CQDs. This indicates that effective electron coupling occurs between Ru and Ir and that electrons may be transferred from Ru to Ir, resulting in the formation of positive and negative charges, i.e. high-valent and low-valent sites, in Ru and Ir, respectively. The variation in BE of Ru and Ir can be attributed to the distinct charge properties and redox characteristics of RuO2@CQDs and IrO2@CQDs. Specifically, RuO2@CQDs exhibit a positive charge along with reducing properties, whereas IrO2@CQDs carry a negative charge and possess oxidizing properties. Consequently, this leads to an increase in the BE of Ru and a decrease in the BE of Ir. The change in the valent state reduces the affinity of the Ru site for positively charged H2 intermediates while enhancing the binding ability of the Ir site to these intermediates. This optimisation of H2 intermediate BE in HER improves the catalytic performance. Compared with RuO2-IrO2, the Ru 3d BE RuO2-IrO2@CQDs moves in the positive direction. Moreover, compared with RuO2-IrO2, the Ir 4f BE of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs is also moving in the positive direction. These results indicate that the introduction of the CQDs induces charge redistribution in the catalyst, possibly due to the rich functional groups on the surface of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(f), the O 1s spectrum of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs can be deconvolved into three peaks, attributable to lattice oxygen, hydroxyl, and adsorbed oxygen, respectively [46]. Notably, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs exhibit a larger proportion of hydroxyl groups than RuO2@CQDs and IrO2@CQDs, which promotes catalytic activity in OER and prevents excessive catalyst dissolution in acidic media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs towards acidic OER
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic activity of the developed catalysts against OER and HER was evaluated via linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a sweep rate of 5 mV/s in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution using a standard three-electrode system. Com. IrO2 and 20% Pt/C catalysts were used as references for comparison.

For OER (Fig. 2 a), RuO2-IrO2@CQDs showed an outstanding overpotential of 180 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, indicating excellent OER activity; moreover, this overpotential is considerably lower than that of RuO2@CQDs (222 mV), IrO2@CQDs (311 mV), and Com. IrO2 (367 mV). The Tafel slope (Fig. S9 and Fig. 2g) of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs (63.86 mV/dec) was considerably lower than that of Com. IrO2 (145.38 mV/dec), RuO2@CQDs (67.53 mV/dec), and IrO2@CQDs (94.49 mV/dec), indicating faster OER kinetics of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs. This disparity in performance is intricately linked to the formation of the CQDs-coupled RuO2-IrO2 heterojunction. The electrochemical active surface areas (ECSA) of these electrocatalysts were also qualitatively evaluated using a double-layer capacitance (C dl) derived from cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the voltage range of 0.8–0.9 V (vs. Reversible Hydrogen Electrode, RHE) at different scanning rates (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 2(c left), RuO2-IrO2@CQDs has a higher OER ECSA (2471.25 cm2) than Com. IrO2, RuO2@CQDs, and IrO2@CQDs, indicating that the density of active sites can be greatly increased by forming a heterostructure coupled by CQDs. The interfacial charge transfer ability of the catalysts was further evaluated via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, Figs. S11 and S12); the resistance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs was found to be considerably smaller than that of RuO2@CQDs, IrO2@CQDs, and Com. IrO2, confirming rapid electron transfer in OER at RuO2-IrO2@CQDs. Moreover, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs offers a higher turnover frequency (TOF) value normalised to the precious metal sites per geometric area (0.041 s−1, η = 200 mV) and higher mass activity (0.04 A/mg, η = 200 mV) than the comparison catalysts, indicating the excellent intrinsic activity of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs (Fig. 2b, c right). Because the calculated TOF values and mass activities are based on all the metal sites in the catalysts, the true TOF values and mass activities should be considerably higher than the theoretical TOF values and mass activities. Furthermore, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalysts with different Ir doping amounts were prepared by the same synthesis method. As shown in Fig. S13, a volcano trend of the OER curve can be observed, according to which the activity of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs with 15 mg of Ir content is the highest. Notably, the LSV curve for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs obtained after 10,000 CV test cycles exhibited negligible attenuation compared with the initial results in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution (Fig. S14). Furthermore, the dynamic impedance evolution of catalysts was observed via in situ EIS tests in the OER potential region. The Bode plot in Fig. 2(d) shows that with the increase of bias voltage, the surface charge distribution of the catalysts is uneven, and the smaller the phase angle in the low frequency region, the faster the OER kinetics. Notably, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs have a narrower transition peak interval (1.25–1.45 V) than RuO2@CQDs (1.25–1.5 V) and IrO2@CQDs (1.25–1.6 V) (Fig. 2e, f), indicating rapid surface deprotonation and electron transfer in the OER at RuO2-IrO2@CQDs. The OER performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs surpasses that of most acidic OER catalysts reported in recent years (Fig. 2h).

In order to reveal the source of high OER performance of the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs electrocatalyst, in situ attenuated total refraction surface-enhanced infrared spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS IR) and differential electrochemical mass spectrometry (DEMS) tests were carried out to determine the catalyst intermediates and gaseous oxygen products produced during the OER process, and then determine the OER mechanism. Isotopic-labelling in situ measurements were performed to elucidate the OER path for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, in which the collected gaseous O2 products were analysed via gas chromatography. In particular, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs were first 18O-labelled by multiple LSV cycles (1.2–1.55 V vs. RHE) in 0.5 M H2SO4 prepared with H2 18O. The 18O-labelled RuO2-IrO2@CQDs was washed several times with deionised water (H2 16O), and the labelling was continued for four LSV cycles in 0.5 M H2SO4 formulated with H2 16O. As shown in Fig. 3 (a and b), when the OER zone was reached, 32O2 and 34O2 signals were detected and 36O2 was not detected. Notably, in the OER region, the signal intensity ratio of 34O2 to 32O2 is approximately 0.7% (Fig. 3c), which is higher than the ratio of 34O2 to 32O2 in natural water (0.2%) [47], indicating that the OER path for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs may belong to LOM, and lattice oxygen directly participates in the O2 molecule formation. Furthermore, in situ ATR-SEIRAS IR tests were performed to confirm the OER path. Fig. 3(d) shows a schematic of the in situ ATR-SEIRAS IR. Fig. 3(e) shows the in situ ATR-SEIRAS IR spectrum of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs under step voltage. When the voltage reaches the OER region, a distinct peak is observed at 1049 cm−1, attributable to the *OO– intermediates formed in the LOM reaction path [48]. Notably, the peak observed at 1049 cm−1 for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs is intensified with increasing voltage, indicating a strengthened interaction between the metal and *OO–. The in situ DEMS and ATR-SEIRAS IR test results demonstrate that the OER path of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs is a LOM path (Fig. 3f), which bypasses the generation of OOH* intermediates, allowing electrophilic lattice oxygen to participate in OER, thereby reducing the limiting energy barrier and enhancing the OER activity. To further verify the mechanism followed by RuO2-IrO2@CQDs in the OER process, 0.1 M tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH) was added to 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, as shown in Fig. S15. As seen, the OER activity of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs was decreased significantly when 0.1 M TMAOH was added in the electrolyte, suggesting that TMA+ can strongly bind to the negative oxygen species produced by the catalyst during OER operation and impede the LOM pathway. Raman spectra in Fig. S15(b) recorded the features of TMA+ of initial RuO2-IrO2@CQDs and RuO2-IrO2@CQDs undergoing OER operation in an electrolyte with or without TMAOH. These results strongly indicate the strong binding of TMA+ with the negative oxygenated species produced by RuO2-IrO2@CQDs during OER process, which impedes the LOM pathway.

The theoretical calculation results (Fig. 3g and h) show that for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, the energy barrier of the rate-determining step (RDS) in the LOM pathway was only 2.36 eV, smaller than that in AEM (2.55 eV). Therefore, theoretical studies showed that RuO2-IrO2@CQDs can improve its OER activity through the LOM pathway, confirming the feasibility of the LOM pathway, which was completely consistent with our experimental observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs towards acidic HER
ce:section.ce:para: The HER performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs was also investigated under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), RuO2-IrO2@CQDs exhibits excellent HER performance, and only 15 mV is needed to achieve a current density of 10 mA/cm2, better than that of 20% Pt/C (26 mV), RuO2@CQDs (128 mV), and IrO2@CQDs (351 mV). The HER activity of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs is notably higher than that of RuO2@CQDs and IrO2@CQDs, indicating that the CQDs-coupled RuO2-IrO2 heterostructure is the main contributor to the excellent electrocatalytic performance. Furthermore, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs have a good Tafel slope (11.2 mV/dec), lower than 20% Pt/C (13.8 mV/dec), RuO2@CQDs (13 mV/dec), and IrO2@CQDs (40.9 mV/dec) (Fig. 4d, e), indicating that the catalyst follows the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [49]. In addition, the ECSAs of all electrocatalysts were qualitatively evaluated using C dl derived from CV in the voltage range of −0.1–0 V (vs. RHE) at different scanning rates (Fig. 4e and Fig. S16). As shown in Fig. 4(c left), RuO2-IrO2@CQDs have a higher ECSA (1701.75 cm2) than 20% Pt/C, RuO2@CQDs, and IrO2@CQDs, indicating that the number of active sites is effectively increased by forming the CQDs-coupled heterostructure. Moreover, the HER performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalysts with different Ir doping amounts was measured. As shown in Fig. S17, a volcano trend of the HER activity curve can be observed, according to which the activity of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs with 15 mg of Ir usage is the highest. Fig. 4(f) and Fig. S18 show the low interfacial charge transfer resistance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, indicating the rapid electron transfer of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs in the HER process. In addition, the TOF value (Fig. 4b) of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs was 0.19 s−1 (η = 10 mV), which is higher than that of RuO2@CQDs (0.07 s−1) and IrO2@CQDs (0.01 s−1) but slightly lower than that of 20% Pt/C (0.5 s−1). In terms of mass activity (Fig. 4c right), RuO2-IrO2@CQDs reached 0.02 A/mg (η = 10 mV), which is higher than RuO2@CQDs (0.006 A/mg) and IrO2@CQDs (0.001 A/mg) and comparable with 20% Pt/C (0.03 A/mg). Notably, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs have outperformed most of the acidic HER catalysts reported in recent years (Fig. 4h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall water splitting in acidic media
ce:section.ce:para: An H-type double-electrode electrolytic cell was assembled using the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst as the anode and cathode and 0.5 M H2SO4 as the electrolyte. To better demonstrate the overall water splitting performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, an H-type double-electrode electrolyser comprising 20% Pt/C-IrO2 was assembled under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), to obtain a current density of 10 mA/cm2, RuO2-IrO2@CQDs require only 1.43 V, which is considerably lower than 20% Pt/C-IrO2 (1.62 V). Moreover, in situ EIS tests were performed in the OER potential region to demonstrate the rapid dynamics of the double-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 5(b and c), the transition interval of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs is 1.4–1.5 V, which is considerably narrower than that of 20% Pt/C-IrO2 (1.4–1.6 V), indicating the efficient and fast electron transfer capability of the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs double-electrode system. Furthermore, the stability of the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs double-electrode system was evaluated by chronopotentiometry. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs double-electrode electrolytic cell operated stably at 10 mA/cm2 for 120 h, and the potential rise rate was only 0.641 mV/h. These results show that the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs double-electrode electrolyser is far superior to the 20% Pt/C-IrO2 double-electrode electrolyser and recently reported double-electrode electrolysers (Fig. 5e). To determine the degradation mechanism of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs during prolonged operation, a 24-hour chronopotentiometry test for overall water splitting (Fig. S19) was conducted. Moreover, the TEM images, HRTEM images, XPS spectra, and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) results were systematically used to carefully analyze the microstructure, surface chemical composition, and loss of active sites of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs after the reaction. As shown in Figs. S20 and S21, the XPS spectra show that, compared with the initial state of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, the BE of both Ru and Ir in anode and cathode moves forward after overall water splitting. For the anode, Ru and Ir move forward by 0.69 and 0.79 eV, respectively, compared to the initial state. For the cathode, Ru and Ir move forward by 0.73 and 0.79 eV, respectively, compared to the initial state. The BE shifts substantiate the emergence of a high-priced Ru/Ir site with higher catalytic activity after overall water splitting. As shown in Figs. S22 and S23. TEM images, HRTEM images, and EDX spectra reveal that RuO2-IrO2@CQDs maintain a complete appearance with RuO2-IrO2 heterostructures and uniform element distribution after overall water splitting, confirming its structural stability. As shown in Fig. S24, the ICP-MS results show that only weak metal dissolution was found for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs after 24-hour of overall water splitting. In particular, for the anode part, the average Ru and Ir losses of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs are only 0.146 and 0.028 µg/h, and for the cathode part, the average Ru and Ir losses of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs are only 0.019 and 0.009 µg/h. These results strongly suggest that the CQDs-coupled RuO2-IrO2 heterojunction is stable during the overall water splitting operation, and prove that the main pathway of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs degradation is the small amount of metal dissolution during the operation.

Encouraged by stable structure and high overall water splitting performance of RuO2-IrO2@CQDs, a proton exchange membrane water electrolysis (PEMWE) device was assembled using RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst as anode and cathode, respectively. As seen in the Fig. 5(f), the PEMWE exhibits excellent performance, with only 1.64 and 1.82 V to achieve 1 and 2 A/cm2, respectively. More importantly, the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst also shows good stability and the PEMWE device can operate steadily for 200 hat 1 A/cm2 without significant degradation (Fig. 5g). Therefore, the excellent performance of PEM electrolyzer based on RuO2-IrO2@CQDs further demonstrates the large application potential of the RuO2-IrO2@CQDs catalyst.

In summary, we reported that the CQD-coupled RuO2-IrO2 heterostructure allows for highly active and stable bifunctional HER-OER electrocatalysis. Overpotentials of only 180 and 15 mV were achieved for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs to drive OER and HER, respectively, at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. RuO2-IrO2@CQDs exhibited extremely high electrochemical specific surface areas of 2471.25 and 1701.75 cm2 for oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions, respectively. Furthermore, the H-type dual-electrode electrolyser comprising RuO2-IrO2@CQDs could run stably for 120 h at a density current of 10 mA/cm2 in an acidic electrolyte. In situ ATR-SEIRAS IR and DEMS measurements revealed that the OER path for RuO2-IrO2@CQDs is a LOM path. This path bypasses the generation of OOH* intermediates, allowing electrophilic lattice oxygen to participate in the OER process, thereby reducing the limiting energy barrier and enhancing the OER activity. Moreover, in situ EIS experiments confirmed that the CQD-coupled heterostructure increased the electron transport rate on the catalyst surface. These findings demonstrate that the heterogeneous structure between RuO2 and IrO2 based on CQDs coupling exhibits the enhanced overall water splitting performance under acidic conditions.

Yuwen Su: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Han Wu: Validation. Siyang Wang: Validation. Zhiang Hu: Validation. Jian Li: Validation. Jiangwei Chang: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Guangchao Yin: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Siyu Lu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ZR2024MB087, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52122308, 51973200, 52202050, and 21905253), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (202300410372), the Joint Fund of Science and Technology R&D Plan of Henan Province (232301420042) and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022TQ0286). We also acknowledge the Center for Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing of Zhengzhou University for supporting this project.

ce:section.ce:para: All the data for this study can be found in the article and Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2025.02.050.

ce:section.ce:para:
